title_en: The Pre-Islamic Prophets and Ancient Nations
content_en:
The second major section of Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh covers the long sweep of prophetic history from Nuh (Noah) through Ibrahim (Abraham), Musa (Moses), Dawud (David), Sulayman (Solomon), and Isa (Jesus). Ibn al-Athir treats these figures not as subjects of legend but as historical actors whose missions shaped civilizations and whose communities' responses to prophetic guidance determined their fates.

Nuh and the Flood

Ibn al-Athir's account of Nuh draws on Quranic narrative and hadith, supplemented by traditions about the construction of the ark, its dimensions, and the geography of the flood. He records debates among scholars over whether the flood was universal or regional, presenting the majority opinion that it covered the known world while noting dissenting views. The account of the ark's resting on Mount Judi (al-Judi), mentioned in the Quran, receives careful treatment.

The section then addresses the dispersal of Nuh's descendants and the founding of the major nations and language groups of the ancient world. Ibn al-Athir connects these genealogies to the origins of Semitic, Hamitic, and Japhetic peoples, providing a framework for understanding the subsequent histories of Mesopotamia, Egypt, Persia, and Arabia.

Ibrahim and the Founding of Makkah

The narrative of Ibrahim occupies considerable space, reflecting his centrality to the Islamic worldview as the father of monotheism. Ibn al-Athir narrates Ibrahim's birth in Babylon, his confrontation with Nimrod, his migration, his trials, and above all his journey to the Arabian Peninsula with his wife Hajar and son Ismail. The founding of the Zamzam well and the construction of the Kaaba with Ismail are presented as pivotal events in sacred geography.

Ibn al-Athir also traces the lineage from Ibrahim through Ismail to the Arab tribes, and through Ishaq (Isaac) to the Israelite prophets. This dual lineage, leading respectively to Muhammad and to Moses and Jesus, is framed as providential preparation for the final revelation.

The Israelite Prophets

The histories of Yusuf (Joseph), Musa, Harun (Aaron), Dawud, and Sulayman are narrated in sequence, with attention to the political and social contexts of each. Ibn al-Athir's account of Musa includes the Exodus, the wandering in the wilderness, the giving of the Torah, and the conquest of Canaan under his successor. He notes the multiple miracles attributed to Musa while maintaining a tone of historical gravity rather than hagiographic embellishment.

The Ancient Civilizations

Interwoven with the prophetic narratives are accounts of the great ancient civilizations: the Pharaonic dynasties of Egypt, the empires of Assyria and Babylon, the kingdom of Persia, and the Hellenistic world. Ibn al-Athir uses these as backdrop to the prophetic missions, showing how the peoples who rejected guidance met their ends while communities that accepted it flourished. This comparative historical method gives the early volumes of Al-Kamil a depth unusual even among the great Islamic chronicles.
title_id: Para Nabi Pra-Islam dan Bangsa-Bangsa Kuno
content_id:
Bagian kedua dari Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh mencakup rentang panjang sejarah kenabian, mulai dari Nuh (as) hingga Ibrahim (as), Musa (as), Dawud (as), Sulayman (as), dan Isa (as). Ibn al-Athir memperlakukan tokoh-tokoh ini bukan sebagai subjek legenda, melainkan sebagai pelaku sejarah yang misinya membentuk peradaban manusia dan mempersiapkan jalan bagi wahyu terakhir kepada Nabi Muhammad ﷺ.

Setiap nabi dipresentasikan dalam konteks umat dan zamannya. Kisah Nuh (as) mencakup pembangunan bahtera dan banjir besar yang memusnahkan kaum yang ingkar. Kisah Ibrahim (as) meliputi perdebatannya dengan kaum penyembah berhala, hijrahnya ke Kan'an dan kemudian ke Mesir, serta pembangunan Ka'bah bersama putranya Ismail (as) — yang menjadi titik awal hubungan langsung antara sejarah kenabian dan tempat-tempat suci Islam.

Narasi tentang Musa (as) menempati porsi yang sangat besar, mencakup kelahirannya di Mesir, pengasuhannya di istana Fir'aun, wahyu di Sinai, keluarnya Bani Israil dari Mesir (al-Isra'), dan pengembaran di padang pasir. Ibn al-Athir memperhatikan secara khusus pelajaran-pelajaran moral dan teologis dari setiap peristiwa, bukan sekadar mencatatnya sebagai fakta historis.

Bagian ini juga membahas bangsa-bangsa kuno seperti kaum 'Ad, Tsamud, dan Madyan yang ditimpa azab karena menolak para nabi yang diutus kepada mereka. Ibn al-Athir menjadikan kisah-kisah ini sebagai cermin bagi pembacanya, mengingatkan bahwa kejayaan dan kehancuran sebuah bangsa sangat bergantung pada kepatuhannya kepada Allah ﷻ.

Kisah Isa (as) menutup bagian ini dengan pemaparan tentang kelahiran ajaibnya, risalahnya kepada Bani Israil, pengangkatannya oleh Allah ﷻ, serta perbedaan pandangan antara Islam dan Kristen mengenai hakikatnya — sebuah pembahasan yang penting mengingat konteks Perang Salib yang menjadi latar penulisan kitab ini.
content_ar: The ثانياً major الفصل of Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh covers the long sweep of prophetic history from Nuh (نوح) through إبراهيم ﷺ (إبراهيم), موسى ﷺ (موسى), داود ﷺ (داود), سليمان ﷺ (سليمان), and عيسى (عيسى ﷺ). Ibn al-Athir treats these figures not as subjects of legend but as historical actors whose missions shaped civilizations and whose communities' responses to prophetic الهداية determined their fates. Nuh and the Flood Ibn al-Athir's account of Nuh draws on القرآني narrative and الحديث, supplemented by traditions about the construction of the ark, its dimensions, and the geography of the flood. He records debates among العلماء over whether the flood was universal or regional, presenting the majority opinion that it covered the known الدنيا بينما noting dissenting views. The account of the ark's resting on Mount Judi (al-Judi), mentioned in the القرآن الكريم, receives careful treatment. The الفصل then addresses the dispersal of Nuh's descendants and the founding of the major nations and language groups of the ancient الدنيا. Ibn al-Athir connects these genealogies to the origins of Semitic, Hamitic, and Japhetic peoples, providing a framework for الفهم the subsequent histories of Mesopotamia, Egypt, Persia, and Arabia. إبراهيم ﷺ and the Founding of مكة المكرمة The narrative of إبراهيم ﷺ occupies considerable space, reflecting his centrality to the Islamic worldview as the الأب of monotheism. Ibn al-Athir narrates إبراهيم ﷺ's birth in Babylon, his confrontation with Nimrod, his migration, his الابتلاءات, and above all his journey to the Arabian Peninsula with his الزوجة Hajar and son Ismail. The founding of the Zamzam well and the construction of the الكعبة المشرفة with Ismail are presented as pivotal events in sacred geography. Ibn al-Athir أيضاً traces the lineage from إبراهيم ﷺ through Ismail to the Arab tribes, and through Ishaq (Isaac) to the Israelite الأنبياء. This dual lineage, leading respectively to محمد ﷺ and to موسى and عيسى ﷺ, is framed as providential preparation for the final الوحي. The Israelite الأنبياء The histories of يوسف ﷺ (يوسف), موسى ﷺ, Harun (Aaron), داود ﷺ, and سليمان ﷺ are narrated in sequence, with attention to the political and social contexts of each. Ibn al-Athir's account of موسى ﷺ includes the Exodus, the wandering in the wilderness, the giving of the Torah, and the conquest of Canaan under his successor. He notes the multiple miracles attributed to موسى ﷺ بينما maintaining a tone of historical gravity rather than hagiographic embellishment. The Ancient Civilizations Interwoven with the prophetic narratives are accounts of the great ancient civilizations: the Pharaonic dynasties of Egypt, the empires of Assyria and Babylon, the kingdom of Persia, and the Hellenistic الدنيا.
